macro_segments.title: Opening remarks on Kohanim and Leviim stations, and non-Jewish access to the Temple

micro_segments.title: Status of single Kohen per station vs. Rambam's view of edah (collective) of Kohanim
Non-Jewish access to the Temple: restrictions in Ezrat Nashim and beyond the Cheil
Tumeh Meis and Ba'al Keri restrictions on Har Habayit; why Ba'al Keri was sent outside

macro_segments.title: The Leviim's twenty-one watching stations: Mishna in Middot and the Gemara's derivation from Divrei Hayamim

micro_segments.title: Parallel Mishna in Middot describing five gates on Har Habayit and five gates of the Azarah with Kohanim and Leviim stations
Gemara asks: Mina hani milei—where do we derive the twenty-one watching locations from?
Rav Yehuda from Sura cites Divrei Hayamim listing Leviim stations: six east, four north, four south, and distribution to Parbar
Calculating the total: twenty-four from Divrei Hayamim; Abaye reinterprets Shnaim Shnaim as just two; adjusting for Parbar reduces to twenty-one
Alternative answer: three of the twenty-four were Kohanim, leaving twenty-one for Leviim; Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi explains Kohanim were called Leviim as a subset

macro_segments.title: Why Leviim watched outside the Azarah gates but inside the walls of Har Habayit

micro_segments.title: Gemara asks: why the difference—Har Habayit on inside, Azarah gates on outside?
Answer: on Har Habayit you can sit to rest; in the Azarah only kings of David may sit, so Leviim moved outside the Azarah to have rest opportunity

macro_segments.title: Debate on the actual number of gates in the Azarah: five, seven, or eight

micro_segments.title: Mishna says five gates; Gemara objects: weren't there seven gates in the Azarah?
Abaye answers: there were seven, but two did not require watching if outer ones were guarded; Rosh and Gershom: two outer gates suffice to cover middle one
Rava brings debate of Tanaim: thirteen officers and seven supervisors minimum; Rabbi Natan derives thirteen from thirteen gates (five of Har Habayit plus eight of Azarah)
Gemara concludes: some say five, some say seven, some say eight—a factual debate about gate count and nomenclature

macro_segments.title: Sleeping arrangements of Kohanim in Beit HaMoked: wearing priestly garments and use as pillow

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Kohanim slept in their own clothes, not in Bigdei Kodesh; they took off and folded the garments under their head
Gemara infers: can you derive benefit from Bigdei Kehuna? Objection: using as pillow implies yes
Counter: maybe 'under their head' means 'opposite their head' only, not literally as pillow
Problem of Kilayim (wool and linen): belt likely shatnez; can't use as pillow when not doing service—must mean near head, not under
Alternative: maybe belt was not shatnez for regular Kohen; or if shatnez, maybe Kohanim did not use the belt as pillow
Rav Ashi: Bigdei Kehuna were stiff/hard; even if shatnez underneath multiple blankets, hard fabric won't touch skin, so permitted to sit/sleep on

macro_segments.title: Ba'al Keri who experienced nocturnal emission: exit through tunnel to Mikvah and Beit Kisei (bathroom)

micro_segments.title: Gemara: if one became Ba'al Keri, he would go through tunnel, immerse in Mikvah, wait until morning
Supports Rabbi Yochanan: tunnels were not consecrated—otherwise why use them?
Ba'al Keri is sent out of two camps (Machaneh Leviyah) like a Zav, hence even on Har Habayit he is prohibited; tunnel exit shows the requirement
After immersion, Ba'al Keri becomes Tevul Yom; rabbinic restriction on Har Habayit allows him to return until morning
Candles lit both sides of tunnel; Beit Kisei had door (Beit Kisei Shel Kavod) for privacy—locked door signals occupancy

macro_segments.title: Story of Rav Safra in bathroom: invited Rav Abba to enter, criticized for acting like a Seirite despite Temple protocol

micro_segments.title: Rav Abba signals with snort; Rav Safra invites him in while using bathroom—breach of modesty protocol
Rav Abba rebukes: our Mishna teaches locked door means someone is there; Seirite behavior unacceptable
Rav Safra's defense: fear of danger (health concern) overrides Kavod; willing to suspend modesty for safety

macro_segments.title: Talmudic teachings on bathroom practices: timing, modesty, hygiene, and health

micro_segments.title: Gemara cites Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel: withholding defecation leads to hemorrhoids; withholding urination leads to jaundice
Rav's teaching to Chiya, Rav Huna to Rabbah: go at nightfall and early morning when no one around—safety and convenience
Rules: uncover only after sitting; cover before standing; always maintain modesty; avoid long trips into forest
Water cup: rinse before use and after use; when sharing cup, first spill some to remove spit from drinker's lips
Story: student refused cup without spillage and died of thirst—therefore rule became mandatory
Rav Ashi: spilling water in front of master is not disrespect; done for cleanliness, not contempt for Torah scholar
